seed: rage vs calm; signal_vs_noise; eroded_symmetry; zigzag; ring against quiet_field; corner_sparks; contained_vs_overflowing; veil; machine_emotion | abstract variation request 427; target batch count 500
title: Overflow vs Containment
reasoning: This artwork captures the emotional tension between rage and calm, represented through a dynamic clash of colors and patterns. The palette, comprising red, black, pale blue, and white, embodies the heat of anger (red) colliding with the soothing quality of calm (pale blue). A diagonal flow begins in the corner, where bright red and black accents erupt, overwhelming the softer blue and white stripes that symbolize controlled calm. The contrast is raw, emphasizing the emotional collision at the top left corner while the calm spreads across the rest of the grid. Broken pixels create an organic disruption, reinforcing the sense of conflict and intensity, manifesting the idea of pressure from one corner that seeks to overflow into the calm, echoing the wrestling sensations of rage and tranquility.

layout.name: pressure_corner
layout.description: One corner is overfilled and the rest of the square reacts with spreading marks.

composition.name: overflow_vs_containment
composition.idea: Bright or hot marks exceed a boundary while cooler marks try to contain them.

referenceExample.title: Rage Entering Calm
referenceExample.pattern: red jagged pixels cutting into blue horizontal bands

generationRules: Pick 1 pattern type for emotion A.
Pick 1 pattern type for emotion B.
Pick 1 shape seed as the central object.
Apply 1 to 6 broken pixels to create imperfection.
Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the mood is panic, rage, urgency, euphoria, or pressure.
Use the shape seed as an abstract organizing metaphor, not as a literal icon.
Make the two selected pattern types visibly collide, overlap, interrupt, or trade space.
Prefer 4 to 8 distinct hex colors, with at least one quiet field color and one accent color.
Keep the 8x8 grid readable at thumbnail size by preserving clear large-scale structure.
Let one emotion dominate 55 to 70 percent of the square unless the selected layout demands balance.
Use broken pixels as intentional disruptions, scars, repairs, leaks, or false echoes.
Avoid perfectly even distribution unless the chosen composition is control, serenity, clarity, or certainty.
Give the main collision a location: center, edge, corner, diagonal, border, or channel.
generationSpec.negativeSpaceRule: Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the chosen emotional tone needs dense pressure.
